title: Cedar Propane Tank Cover Table
summary: A slatted cedar box that hides the grill's propane tank and works as a side table — hinged lid on top, and gaps everywhere because a gas tank must breathe.
description:
Every deck with a grill has the same eyesore: the spare propane tank. This build disguises it as a small cedar side table — slatted sides over two 2x4 frames, a hinged slat lid for swapping tanks, and a top sturdy enough to park a tray of burgers on.

The design detail that matters is the one that looks like sloppiness: the slats have gaps, and the box has no bottom. That's not rustic styling — it's ventilation. Propane is heavier than air, and a leaking tank inside a sealed box turns a side table into a slow hazard. Build it slatted and open-bottomed like the plan says, set it on the deck boards, and never 'improve' it with weatherstripping or a floor. For the same reason, the hinged lid isn't just convenience: you want to lift it and check the valve without wrestling the whole cover off.

Cedar fence pickets keep the cost around fifty dollars, and several cuts are marked 'cut to fit' — picket widths wander enough that the front, back, and lid slats should be measured off your actual assembled box, not the cut list. Measure your tank first, too: this fits the standard 20 lb cylinder with room to lift it out.

tools.note: Several pieces are cut-to-fit off the assembled box — measure as you go, not all up front.
Pickets are rough-sawn; the top especially needs smoothing if it's going to serve as a table.

materials.name: Cedar fence picket, 5-1/2" x 72", not dog-eared
materials.note: All the slats — sides, front, back, and lid.

materials.name: 1x3, scrap
materials.note: 31" total — inside lid supports. Any 3/4" scrap works.

materials.name: Exterior screws, #8 x 1-1/4", self-tapping, star drive
Exterior screws, #9 x 2-1/2", self-tapping, star drive
Piano hinge or exterior hinges
materials.note: Exterior-rated — plain steel hinges rust-streak cedar in a season.

cutList.note: Cut to fit your assembled box — picket widths vary.

steps.title: Build the two leg frames
steps.body: Screw each leg cap across the tops of two legs with the 2-1/2" screws, making two identical U-frames. These are the box's whole skeleton — check them for square, because every slat that follows registers off them.
materials: Exterior screws, #9 x 2-1/2", self-tapping, star drive

steps.title: Slat the side panels
steps.body: Attach four side slats to each frame with the 1-1/4" screws, leaving a 3/4" overhang at the top — the plan's trick is to use the 1x3 lid-support stock as a spacer gauge for that overhang. Keep the slat gaps even; they're doing the ventilation work.
materials: Cedar fence picket, 5-1/2" x 72", not dog-eared
Exterior screws, #8 x 1-1/4", self-tapping, star drive

steps.title: Close the front and back
steps.body: Stand the two slatted sides up and screw the front and back slats across them — measure the actual box for these, cutting to fit rather than trusting the list. If you want the notch for the grill's hose at the front, mark and cut it now.
materials: Cedar fence picket, 5-1/2" x 72", not dog-eared
Exterior screws, #8 x 1-1/4", self-tapping, star drive

steps.title: Start the lid: outer slats
steps.body: Lay the 1x3 supports on top of the leg caps, centered, and set the two outer lid slats on them flush with the box's outside edges. Screw the slats to the 1x3s — not to the box — with the 1-1/4" screws. You're building the lid in place so it fits this box, not a theoretical one.
materials: Cedar fence picket, 5-1/2" x 72", not dog-eared
Exterior screws, #8 x 1-1/4", self-tapping, star drive

steps.title: Fill the lid center
steps.body: Set the remaining lid slat in the middle, gap it evenly, and screw it to the supports. Lift the finished lid off and sand it well — this is the table surface, the part hands and drinks actually touch.
materials: Cedar fence picket, 5-1/2" x 72", not dog-eared
Exterior screws, #8 x 1-1/4", self-tapping, star drive

steps.title: Hinge the lid at the back
steps.body:
Attach the lid to the box with the piano hinge (or a pair of exterior hinges) along the back edge. Swap your tank in, check the lid opens past vertical so it stays put while you work the valve, and park the table next to the grill.

Leave the cedar raw to silver, or oil it — but never seal the box up tight. The gaps are the safety feature.

images.alt: Slatted cedar box side table that conceals a propane tank, with hinged lid
